coredata.dc:title: Liquid nitrogen-assisted synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from Blueberry and their performance in Fe3+ detection

Şakiroğlu, Halis
Onganer, Yavuz
Meral, Kadem
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) were synthesized by a facile method containing liquid N2 treatment and centrifuge processes. The photophysical properties of the C-dots in an aqueous solution were examined at various conditions such as concentration, temperature, pH and excitation wavelength by using UV–vis absorption, fluorescence and time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopies. The C-dots emitted a broad fluorescence between approximately 350–550nm and their fluorescence was tuned by changing excitation wavelength. The as-prepared C-dots were applied to Fe3+ detection from aqueous solution. Spectroscopic data revealed that the as-prepared C-dots were used to detect Fe3+ in the range of 12.5μM to 100μM as a fluorescence sensor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence sensor
Fe3+ detection
Blueberry

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis absorption and (b) fluorescence spectra of the C-dots.

(a) Normalized fluorescence spectra of the C-dots at various excitation wavelengths in the range of 300nm to 370nm with 10nm increment, (b) the fluorescence intensity of the C-dots-based excitation wavelength at λmax.
(a) UV–vis absorption and (b) fluorescence spectra of C-dots at various pH values.
(a) UV–vis absorption and (b) fluorescence spectra of C-dots at various temperatures.

Histograms of (F0 −F)/F0, where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the C-dots in the absence and presence of 0.1mM of different metal ions, respectively. The samples are excited at the excitation wavelength of 340nm.
The florescence responses of the C-dots in aqueous solution after the addition of different concentrations of Fe3+ ions.

Fluorescence decay traces of the C-dots in the absence (blank) and presence (red) of 0.91mM Fe3+ (λ ex. =366nm).With reference to the color information in the artwork, a parenthetical sentence, “For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of the article.” has been incorporated in the caption to Fig. 9. Please check. wavelength. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of the article.)
Schematic illustration of the preparation of C-dots and their application for Fe3+ detection.
(a) The fluorescence responses of the C-dots in water after the addition of different concentrations of Fe3+ and (b) plot of F0/F with the concentration of Fe3+.

head.ce:title: Liquid nitrogen-assisted synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from Blueberry and their performance in Fe3+ detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, Ataturk University, 25240 Erzurum, Turkey

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, Ataturk University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) were synthesized by a facile method containing liquid N2 treatment and centrifuge processes. The photophysical properties of the C-dots in an aqueous solution were examined at various conditions such as concentration, temperature, pH and excitation wavelength by using UV–vis absorption, fluorescence and time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopies. The C-dots emitted a broad fluorescence between approximately 350–550nm and their fluorescence was tuned by changing excitation wavelength. The as-prepared C-dots were applied to Fe3+ detection from aqueous solution. Spectroscopic data revealed that the as-prepared C-dots were used to detect Fe3+ in the range of 12.5μM to 100μM as a fluorescence sensor.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanoparticles (C-dots), as a new type of carbon-based nanomaterial, have attracted great attention due to their unique optical/chemical properties [1]. C-dots have potential applications in various fields, e.g. bio-imaging, optoelectronic devices, sensors, drug delivery, antimicrobial activity and photocatalysis. In spite of traditional fluorescent organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, the fluorescent C-dots show distinguished properties such as stable photoluminescence, tunable emission, chemical inertness, aqueous solubility, easy functionalization, good biocompatibility and low toxicity [2–6]. There are several methods for the synthesis of fluorescent C-dots including chemical ablation [6–8], electrochemical [9,10], microware irradiation [3,11,12], ultrasonic [13], pyrolysis [14] and hydrothermal/solvothermal treatment methods [1,4–6,12,15–21]. Nature is a limitless source, which gives material scientists the freedom to have inspiration for new ideas, new ways of developing nanomaterials with novel structures and intriguing properties, and less environmental impact. Recently, major attention has been focused on developing green methods for preparing fluorescent C-dots using natural precursors [1,2,4,7,17,20–22]

In the present study, a green, simple, and low-cost strategy toward highly fluorescent C-dots by sonication of Blueberry, a low-cost and easy-available material, has been reported. A significant advantage of this method is green synthesis, and it does not require severe synthetic conditions (such as strong solvents, surface passivation reagents, and complicated post-treatment process). Most importantly, it is further demonstrated that the C-dots can serve as very effective fluorescent probes for Fe3+ detection. A chemical-free synthesis of fluorescent C-dots with various merits has been reported such as: (a) the process is facile with no complex or post-treatment procedures (b) the starting material is green, low-cost and eco-friendly, and (c) the C-dots exhibit excellent sensitivity and selectivity toward Fe3+. The material is recommended as a great potential for application in the field of sensing and bio-imaging [9,11,13–16,23–25].

Blueberries were picked up from Tokat, Canik Mountains and washed several times with distilled water before preparation of the C-dots. ZnCl2.H2O, NaNO3, AgCl, Pb(CH3COO)2, MnCl2, HgCl2, CuCl2, CdI2, FeCl3 and CrCl3 were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich.

The fluorescent C-dots were prepared with the following procedure: 30g of Blueberries were powdered by the assistance of liquid nitrogen (N2). The obtained powders were transferred into 100mL water and were firmly broken for 30min (ultraturrax at 8500rpm). The mixture was diluted to 500mL by pure water and was sonicated for 3h. Afterwards, the final mixture was centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 30min. The limpid supernatant was obtained and completed by pure water to 500mL. The final solution was called a stock solution and its concentration was 60mg/L at pH 3.32. Desired concentrations of the C-dots were obtained by dilution of the stock solution. 12mg/L of C-dots in aqueous solution was used for its spectroscopic characterization.

Absorption and fluorescence spectra of the samples were recorded by using Perkin Elmer Lambda 35 UV-Vis. spectrophotometer and Shimadzu RF-5301PC spectrofluorophotometer, respectively. The time-resolved measurements were carried out using a Laser Strobe Model TM3 spectrofluorophotometer. Its light source is a combination of pulsed nitrogen laser/tunable dye laser. The pulse width is about 800 ps and has a repetition rate of up to 20 pulses per second. The FTIR measurements were done using a Perkin-Elmer spectrum-1 spectrometer. The powder X-ray diffractograms (XRD) of the samples were collected by using a Rigaku (miniflex) powder X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ=1.5405Å).

20μL of the stock C-dots solution was put into 4mL of pure water, followed by the addition of different concentrations of cations. The as-prepared solutions were thoroughly mixed and left to react at room temperature. Then, their fluorescence responses for cations were recorded.

The water-soluble C-dots were simply prepared by facile one-step treatment of Blueberries at room temperature. The most facile method for the characterization of C-dots is the investigation of their absorption and fluorescence properties. The absorption and fluorescence spectra of the C-dots in aqueous solution were given in Fig. 1 .

The absorption spectrum exhibited two peaks around 283 and 325nm as seen Fig. 1a The absorption band at 283nm was ascribed to π–π* transition of aromatic CC bonds, while an absorption shoulder at 325nm was attributed to n–π* transition of CO bonds, which carboxyl groups were located on the surface of the C-dots and CC groups were available in the core of C-dots [26]. The C-dots demonstrated an intense fluorescence band appeared at 450nm when they were excited at 340nm. This type of fluorescence band is generally observed for C-dots. The fluorescence band of the C-dots observed in the range of 350nm to 550nm depended on the excitation wavelength, sensitively (Fig. 2 ).

When the excitation wavelength was changed in the range of 300–370nm with 10nm increment, the fluorescence maximum of the C-dots in water was shifted from 444nm to 454nm as seen from Fig. 2. The excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence intensity was extensively reported on the fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials, and it may be resulted from the optical selection of differently sized particles and surface defects of particles. The fluorescence intensity of as-prepared C-dots depended on the excitation wavelength was shown in Fig. 2a Firstly, the fluorescence intensity of C-dots increased and then it decreased.

Additionally, the as-prepared C-dots were characterized by using XRD and FTIR spectroscopy. Fig. 3 demonstrated XRD pattern of the C-dots. The important spectral data is XRD to characterize the C-dots showing a broad peak centered at 2θ∼24°, corresponding to amorphous carbon ordered in a considerably random fashion. The XRD pattern of as-prepared C-dots given in Fig. 3 showed a weak peak at 2θ∼40° corresponding to the d (100) plane. The poor crystalline nature of the C-dots indicates the presence of more oxygen-containing functional groups [3].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temperature and pH-dependent absorption and fluorescence characteristics of C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of the C-dots at various pH and temperatures is significant for their application in many fields. In this regard, the absorption and fluorescence properties of the C-dots at various pH values (2–13) were investigated (Fig. 5a)

The solution pH was adjusted by adding concentrated HCl and NaOH. The absorption spectra of the C-dots in the range of pH from 2 to 7 were identical. When the solution pH was increased from 7 to 13, the absorption spectra of the C-dots changed compared to those in the acidic pH values. The absorption band of the C-dots in the basic pH values appeared at about 375nm while it was observed at 325nm in the range of pH from 2 to 7. The fluorescence spectra of the C-dots at various pH values were given in Fig. 5b. The fluorescence intensity of the C-dots decreased by increasing pH and its maximum changed slightly. Briefly, the satisfactory fluorescence response was obtained from the C-dots in all pH values.

The temperature-dependent absorption and fluorescent measurements of the C-dots between 3 and 60°C were conducted (Fig. 6 ). The absorption spectra of the C-dots given in Fig. 6a were nearly similar at all temperatures. Minimal changes in the absorption spectrum of the C-dots showed their stability in the wide temperature range. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity of the C-dots decreased by increasing temperature (Fig. 6b) [26]. This was explained due to the enhancement in the nonradiative transitions with the temperature increasing. Consequently, the stability of the C-dots at a wide pH and temperature range suggests that they are appropriate for numerous biological applications.

The selectivity of the C-dots were tested by the addition of various metal ions (Ag+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Na+ and Pb2+), on the emission response of the fluorescent probes as presented in Fig. 7 The performed fluorescence study revealed that Fe3+ had the ability to quench the fluorescence intensity of C-dots while other metal ions had a little or no quenching effects. These observations indicated a high selectivity of the present fluorescent probe toward the detection of Fe3+ due to the stronger affinity of Fe3+ than other metal ions. The interaction of the C-dots with Fe3+ ions (in C-dots-Fe3+ complexes) would facilitate charge transfer and restrain the radiative recombination of excitons, leading to distinguished fluorescence quenching effects.

The as-prepared C-dots were potentially used as a fluorescence sensor to analyze Fe3+ ions, since it was found that Fe3+ ions quenched the fluorescence of the C-dots in aqueous solution. The fluorescence spectra of the C-dots in the range of 0.48 mM-5.6mM Fe3+ were recorded (Fig. 8 ).

As seen from Fig. 8 fluorescence of the C-dots was determined to be completely quench the presence of 4.3mM Fe3+. Fluorescence lifetime measurements were conducted to gain further insight into the mechanism of fluorescence quenching of the C-dots by Fe3+. To calculate the fluorescence lifetimes of the samples, the fluorescence decays of the C-dots in the absence and the presence of Fe3+ were shown in Fig. 9 The fluorescence lifetime of the blank C-dots was calculated to be 4.51ns while it was 3.8ns in the presence of Fe3+. This revealed that the quenching was dynamic, further confirming an ultrafast electron transfer process in the C-dots–Fe3+ system [27].
Taking into account spectroscopic results, the synthesis and Fe3+ detection process of the C-dots were schematized in Scheme 1 .

To evaluate the sensitivity of this system, various concentrations of Fe3+ within 100μM were studied by the fluorescence measurements (Fig. 10 ) As shown in Fig. 10a fluorescence intensity of the C-dots was sensitive to Fe3+ and decreased with increasing Fe3+ concentration. The quenching efficiency is analyzed by the Stern–Volmer equation, F0/F=1+KSV[Q], where KSV is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant, [Q] is the concentration of Fe3+, and F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities at 452nm in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively. Fig. 10b showed a good linearity in the range of 12.5–100μM (R2 =0.98116). KSV was determined as 3.148×103 (R2 =0.99981). The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 9.97μM, which readily meets the Fe3+ detection requirement [28]. LOQ is determined to be 33.23μM.

In summary, a facile, simple, and low-cost method was applied for the green synthesis of the water-soluble C-dots from Blueberry as a carbon source. The absorption and fluorescence properties of the C-dots under various conditions were determined. Spectroscopic data revealed that the fluorescence band maximum of the C-dots was readily tuned depending on the excitation wavelength. Performed fluorescence studies revealed that the as-prepared C-dots were used for the sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ in the wide linear range from 12.5μM to 100μM and the LOD was 9.97μM based on the quenching effects of Fe3+. The quenching effect of Fe3+ was also evaluated by lifetime measurements and it was shown that the presence of Fe3+ in aqueous solution decreased the fluorescence lifetime of the C-dots. The mentioned approach was further extended to the practical assay of Fe3+ in aqueous solution. Due to the easy production of stable and strong fluorescence of the C-dots, we believe that the C-dots would have many potential applications in chemistry and biology.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support by “Atatürk University Scientific Research Project Council” is gratefully acknowledged.
